text:
Musicians contend in every place:
All day, within the thronged expanse,
I hear the silver clash;
And—waking long before the dawn—
Such rapture sweeps across the town
I think it says, “new life!”
source_text:
Musicians wrestle everywhere:
All day, among the crowded air,
I hear the silver strife;
And -- waking long before the dawn --
Such transport breaks upon the town
I think it that "new life!"

text:
Often have I joined your social band,  
And passed the night in cheer and play;  
Often, with high command in hand,  
I ruled the sons of light away:  
And by that bright hieroglyphic sign,  
Seen only by the Craftsman’s eye,  
Strong Memory shall on my heart enshrine  
Those happy scenes, when far am I.
source_text:
Oft have I met your social band,
And spent the cheerful, festive night;
Oft, honour’d with supreme command,
Presided o’er the sons of light:
And by that hieroglyphic bright,
Which none but Craftsmen ever saw
Strong Mem’ry on my heart shall write
Those happy scenes, when far awa.

text:
I am the crushed fireman, my breastbone shattered,  
Walls came down and entombed me in their wreckage,  
I breathed in heat and smoke, heard my comrades crying out,  
Heard from far off the sharp clink of picks and shovels,  
Now the beams are lifted clear, and I am raised out gently.
source_text:
I am the mash’d fireman with breast-bone broken,
Tumbling walls buried me in their debris,
Heat and smoke I inspired, I heard the yelling shouts of my comrades,
I heard the distant click of their picks and shovels,
They have clear’d the beams away, they tenderly lift me forth.

text:
Her wavering hand upon the balustrade,  
Old Angela was feeling out the stair,  
When Madeline, St. Agnes’ spell-bound maid,  
Rose, like a summoned spirit, unaware:  
With a silver taper’s gleam, and reverent care,  
She turned, and led the ancient gossip down  
To the safe level of the matting there.  
Now brace yourself, young Porphyro, to gaze on that bed;  
She comes, she comes again, like a ring-dove scared and fled.
source_text:
Her falt'ring hand upon the balustrade,
Old Angela was feeling for the stair,
When Madeline, St. Agnes' charmed maid,
Rose, like a mission'd spirit, unaware:
With silver taper's light, and pious care,
She turn'd, and down the aged gossip led
To a safe level matting. Now prepare,
Young Porphyro, for gazing on that bed;
She comes, she comes again, like ring-dove fray'd and fled.

text:
A widow-bird sat mournful
Upon a frosty bough.
[SINGS]
Heigh-ho! the lark and the owl!
One greets the morning, and one soothes the night:—
Only the nightingale, poor loving soul,
Keeps singing like a fool through darkness and through light.
source_text:
‘A widow bird sate mourning
Upon a wintry bough.’
[SINGS]
Heigho! the lark and the owl!
One flies the morning, and one lulls the night:—
Only the nightingale, poor fond soul,
Sings like the fool through darkness and light.

text:
Oxen, clattering your yoke and chain, or standing still in the leaf-shadow, what
is it you carry in your eyes?
It feels to me like more than every page I have ever read.
source_text:
Oxen that rattle the yoke and chain or halt in the leafy shade, what
is that you express in your eyes?
It seems to me more than all the print I have read in my life.

text:
For with bold speech I ripped away the veil that hid  
Nature, and Truth, and Liberty, and Love,—  
Like one who, from a mountain’s crest,  
Points to the sun not yet risen!—the shadows own  
His truth, and scatter from each stream and grove.  
So, tender thoughts filled many a heart,—  
Wisdom for many a soul forged the armor of tested love,  
And fierce contempt for evil, three times soaked in molten steel, made the will unbreakable.
source_text:
‘For, with strong speech I tore the veil that hid
Nature, and Truth, and Liberty, and Love,—
As one who from some mountain’s pyramid
Points to the unrisen sun!—the shades approve
His truth, and flee from every stream and grove.
Thus, gentle thoughts did many a bosom fill,—
Wisdom, the mail of tried affections wove
For many a heart, and tameless scorn of ill,
Thrice steeped in molten steel the unconquerable will.

text:
And if my stocking hung so high,  
Would it dim the Christmas cheer,  
That not a single Santa Claus  
Could reach my altitude up here?
source_text:
And if my stocking hung too high,
Would it blur the Christmas glee,
That not a Santa Claus could reach
The altitude of me?

text:
Do you see that fellow, called a lord,  
Who struts about, and stares, and all that?  
Though crowds bow low to every word,  
He’s still a fool for all that:  
For all that, and all that,  
His ribbon, star, and all that:  
The one with an independent mind  
Just looks him over, laughs at all that.
source_text:
Ye see yon birkie, ca’d a lord,
Wha struts, an’ stares, an’ a’ that;
Tho’ hundreds worship at his word,
He’s but a coof for a’ that:
For a’ that, an’ a’ that,
His ribband, star, an’ a’ that:
The man o’ independent mind
He looks an’ laughs at a’ that.

text:
No more for him the storm-tossed clashes of life,  
No more of triumph, no more of defeat—no more time’s shadowed happenings,  
Driving on like endless clouds across the sky.  
But sing, poet, in our name,
source_text:
No more for him life’s stormy conflicts,
Nor victory, nor defeat--no more time’s dark events,
Charging like ceaseless clouds across the sky.
But sing poet in our name,

text:
We strayed to the pine-clad forest  
That fringes the Ocean’s foam,  
The softest wind was in its nest,  
The tempest in its home.
source_text:
We wandered to the Pine Forest
That skirts the Ocean’s foam,
The lightest wind was in its nest,
The tempest in its home.

text:
We danced and leapt the whole long day,
Till even the piper lads were tired and weary;
Yet Charlie still must bear the cost
For kissing Theniel’s bonnie Mary.
Theniel Menzies’ bonnie Mary, &c.
source_text:
We lap a’ danc’d the lee-lang day,
Till piper lads were wae and weary;
But Charlie gat the spring to pay
For kissin Theniel’s bonie Mary.
Theniel Menzies’ bonie Mary, &c.

text:
And the spell of the carol seized me,
As though I were held by my comrades’ hands in the night,
And the voice of my spirit kept time with the bird’s song.
source_text:
And the charm of the carol rapt me,
As I held as if by their hands my comrades in the night,
And the voice of my spirit tallied the song of the bird.

text:
Months have slipped by in lingering circles,
Each night the specter makes its way,
With trembling tread it stirs the ground,
With trembling tread it wanders near me still—
source_text:
Months are passed in lingering round,
Every night the spectre comes,
With thrilling step it shakes the ground,
With thrilling step it round me roams—

text:
Before you had a thought of spring,
save as a guess,
you saw, God bless his quick arrival,
a figure in the sky
in colors all his own,
a little weatherworn,
with garments full of life
in indigo and brown.
source_text:
Before you thought of spring,
Except as a surmise,
You see, God bless his suddenness,
A fellow in the skies
Of independent hues,
A little weather-worn,
Inspiriting habiliments
Of indigo and brown.

text:
I hired a man at Michaelmas,
For three poor pennies’ fee;
But all the fault I found in him,
He could not work nor flee,
O can you work nor flee, &c.
source_text:
I fee’d a man at Michaelmas,
Wi’ airle pennies three;
But a’ the faut I had to him,
He could na labour lea,
O can ye labour lea, &c.

text:
My hands, my limbs grow numb and lifeless,  
My brain is torn and lost in turmoil,  
Though the ancient beams should split apart, I will not let go,  
I will hold thee close, O God, though the waves beat hard against me,  
Thee, thee at least I know.
source_text:
My hands, my limbs grow nerveless,
My brain feels rack’d, bewilder’d,
Let the old timbers part, I will not part,
I will cling fast to Thee, O God, though the waves buffet me,
Thee, Thee at least I know.

text:
This passion raised him to his feet,
and set his hands contending in the air,
his Druid hair to tremble, damp with sweat,
his eyes to blaze and fever, voice gone still.
He stood and did not hear Thea's deep sobs;
a little while, and then once more he seized
his utterance, and spoke.--"But can I not create?
Can I not shape? Can I not bring to birth
another world, another universe,
to break this one and grind it into dust?
Where is another chaos? Where?"--That cry
reached Olympus and made the rebel three
tremble. Thea stirred, and in her look
there was a glimmer of hope, as she spoke
swiftly, though still with awe, as follows.
source_text:
This passion lifted him upon his feet,
And made his hands to struggle in the air,
His Druid locks to shake and ooze with sweat,
His eyes to fever out, his voice to cease.
He stood, and heard not Thea's sobbing deep;
A little time, and then again he snatch'd
Utterance thus.--"But cannot I create?
Cannot I form? Cannot I fashion forth
Another world, another universe,
To overbear and crumble this to nought?
Where is another chaos? Where?"--That word
Found way unto Olympus, and made quake
The rebel three.--Thea was startled up,
And in her bearing was a sort of hope,
As thus she quick-voic'd spake, yet full of awe.

text:
All then was bright, all was glad,
No thought reached past the hour I knew,
I moved within delight I had,
Delight, alas, that faded too, like a flower that bowed and withdrew.
source_text:
All then was jocund, all was gay,
No thought beyond the present hour,
I danced in pleasure’s fading ray,
Fading alas! as drooping flower.

text:
Ghost nor bogle shalt thou dread,  
Thou art by Love and Heav’n led,  
Nothing ill may come thee nigh;  
My bonie Dearie.  
Ca’ the yowes, &c.
source_text:
Ghaist nor bogle shalt thou fear,
Thou’rt to Love and Heav’n sae dear,
Nocht of ill may come thee near;
My bonie Dearie.
Ca’ the yowes, &c.

text:
And you, the emblem flying above it all!  
Delicate beauty, a word for you, perhaps a useful one:  
remember, you have not always been, as now, so comfortably  
enthroned.  
In scenes unlike these, I have watched you falter,  
not so neat and whole, nor newly flowering in folds of  
untainted silk,  
but frayed into bunting, ripped to shreds on your splintered staff,  
or clutched to some young standard-bearer’s chest by frantic hands,  
fiercely contested, for life or death, long fought over,  
through cannon thunder, through curses and groans and shouted cries, and  
rifle volleys snapping hard, through surging ranks like wild demons, lives cast aside,  
for your mere scrap, blackened with soot and smoke and soaked in blood,  
and for that reason, my beauty, so that you might linger now  
safely above us, I have seen many a good man go down.
source_text:
And thou, the Emblem waving over all!
Delicate beauty, a word to thee, (it may be salutary,)
Remember thou hast not always been as here to-day so comfortably
ensovereign’d,
In other scenes than these have I observ’d thee flag,
Not quite so trim and whole and freshly blooming in folds of
stainless silk,
But I have seen thee bunting, to tatters torn upon thy splinter’d staff,
Or clutch’d to some young color-bearer’s breast with desperate hands,
Savagely struggled for, for life or death, fought over long,
’Mid cannons’ thunder-crash and many a curse and groan and yell, and
rifle-volleys cracking sharp,
And moving masses as wild demons surging, and lives as nothing risk’d,
For thy mere remnant grimed with dirt and smoke and sopp’d in blood,
For sake of that, my beauty, and that thou might’st dally as now
secure up there,
Many a good man have I seen go under.

text:
Then Mercury replied with careful speech:—
“You have asked wisely what my art can do;
I grudge you nothing that I know to teach,
not even now—for in both word and will
I would be mild with you; in your wise soul
you can attain all things, and your place
is highest in Heaven among the sons of Jove,
who loves you with the fullness of his love.”
source_text:
To whom thus Mercury with prudent speech:—
‘Wisely hast thou inquired of my skill:
I envy thee no thing I know to teach
Even this day:—for both in word and will
I would be gentle with thee; thou canst reach
All things in thy wise spirit, and thy sill
Is highest in Heaven among the sons of Jove,
Who loves thee in the fulness of his love.

text:
Fasten the threads to my life, my Lord,
Then I am ready to leave!
Only a glance at the horses—
Swift! That will suffice!
source_text:
Tie the strings to my life, my Lord,
Then I am ready to go!
Just a look at the horses --
Rapid! That will do!

text:
The eagle, on the rocky height,  
Keeps you in view as prey in sight;  
Within his breast no pity lies,  
Only stern necessity applies.  
But man, to whom alone is given  
A direct beam from pitying heaven,  
Boasts a heart made kindly, humane—  
And slays the creatures for his pleasure slain!
source_text:
The eagle, from the cliffy brow,
Marking you his prey below,
In his breast no pity dwells,
Strong necessity compels:
But Man, to whom alone is giv’n
A ray direct from pitying Heav’n,
Glories in his heart humane—
And creatures for his pleasure slain!

text:
I move from bed to bed, I lie near the others who sleep  
one by one,  
I dream within my dream all the dreams of the other dreamers,  
And I become the other dreamers.
source_text:
I go from bedside to bedside, I sleep close with the other sleepers
each in turn,
I dream in my dream all the dreams of the other dreamers,
And I become the other dreamers.

text:
But we shall quit this cave of shadow-thronged
And dwell among the Gods, and through each day
Hold lofty converse, sharing all they own
Of lavish wealth and spoil that never fails;
And from the portion that my father gave
To Phoebus, I shall wrest my own away,
Which if my father will not—none the less I,
The robber-king, may still but make the trial.
source_text:
‘But we will leave this shadow-peopled cave
And live among the Gods, and pass each day
In high communion, sharing what they have
Of profuse wealth and unexhausted prey;
And from the portion which my father gave
To Phoebus, I will snatch my share away,
Which if my father will not—natheless I,
Who am the king of robbers, can but try.

text:
For as long as life’s dear blood runs warm,
No thought of her shall ever leave my mind;
Though fairest be the grace of her form,
She holds the truest, kindest heart.
O, know ye wha’s, &c.
source_text:
For while life’s dearest blood is warm,
Ae thought frae her shall ne’er depart,
And she, as fairest is her form,
She has the truest, kindest heart.
O wat ye wha’s, &c.

text:
O book, O chants—must all of this come to only this?
Must we barely make it to this first beginning of ourselves?—and yet it is
enough, O soul;
O soul, we have indeed appeared—that is enough.
source_text:
O book, O chants! must all then amount to but this?
Must we barely arrive at this beginning of us? --and yet it is
enough, O soul;
O soul, we have positively appear’d--that is enough.

text:
I wondered how it would shine in gold  
When Richard rode to mill;  
And then I longed to slip away,  
But some force gripped my will.
source_text:
I thought how yellow it would look
When Richard went to mill;
And then I wanted to get out,
But something held my will.

text:
What marvels have we witnessed  
and heard at the Mermaid—words so quick, so finely lit,  
as though whoever spoke them had poured his whole bright wit  
into a jest, and, having done so,  
resolved to spend the rest  
of his slow, unlovely life  
in folly.
source_text:
What things have we seen
Done at the Mermaid! heard words that have been
So nimble and so full of subtle flame,
As if that any one from whence they came
Had meant to put his whole wit in a jest,
And has resolved to live a fool the rest
Of his dull life.

text:
While briars and honeysuckle bud bright green,  
And partridges cry loudly at evening,  
And morning pussy-willows can be seen,  
my muse is stirred,  
This liberty from an unknown friend,  
I pray you excuse.
source_text:
While briers an’ woodbines budding green,
An’ paitricks scraichin loud at e’en,
An’ morning poussie whiddin seen,
Inspire my muse,
This freedom, in an unknown frien’,
I pray excuse.

text:
Once Paumanok,  
When lilac fragrance filled the air, and Fifth-month grass was rising,  
Along this seashore, amid some briers,  
Two feathered visitors from Alabama, the pair together,  
And their nest, and four pale-green eggs freckled brown,  
And every day the male bird near at hand went to and fro,  
And every day the female bird bent on her nest, silent, bright-eyed,  
And every day I, a curious boy, never too near, never troubling them,  
Carefully peering, taking in, interpreting.
source_text:
Once Paumanok,
When the lilac-scent was in the air and Fifth-month grass was growing,
Up this seashore in some briers,
Two feather’d guests from Alabama, two together,
And their nest, and four light-green eggs spotted with brown,
And every day the he-bird to and fro near at hand,
And every day the she-bird crouch’d on her nest, silent, with bright eyes,
And every day I, a curious boy, never too close, never disturbing
them,
Cautiously peering, absorbing, translating.

text:
He hears the lambs’ unguarded cry,  
And the ewes’ soft answer in return;  
He keeps watch while they rest in peace,  
For they know their shepherd has come near.
source_text:
For he hears the lambs’ innocent call,
And he hears the ewes’ tender reply;
He is watchful while they are in peace,
For they know when their shepherd is nigh.

text:
Gone is the merry morris beat;  
Gone the song of Gamelyn’s heat;  
Gone the hard-girt outlaw lying  
Idle in the “green shaw” sighing;  
All have passed and gone away!  
And if Robin were cast today  
Suddenly from his grassy grave,  
And if Marian were to have  
Once more her days among the trees,  
She would weep, and he would craze:  
He would swear, for all his oaks,  
Felled by dockyard hammer-strokes,  
Had rotted out on salt-sea seas;  
She would weep that all her bees  
Sang no more to her—strange thought!  
Honey cannot be had for naught!
source_text:
Gone, the merry morris din;
Gone, the song of Gamelyn;
Gone, the tough-belted outlaw
Idling in the "grenè shawe;"
All are gone away and past!
And if Robin should be cast
Sudden from his turfed grave,
And if Marian should have
Once again her forest days,
She would weep, and he would craze:
He would swear, for all his oaks,
Fall'n beneath the dockyard strokes,
Have rotted on the briny seas;
She would weep that her wild bees
Sang not to her--strange! that honey
Can't be got without hard money!

text:
The monks, in wonder, stand and shiver still,  
They break through the chancel’s shadowed gloom;  
From St. Edmond’s shrine, behold—a skeleton hand  
Points toward the black marble tomb.
source_text:
The Monks in amazement shuddering stand,
They burst through the chancel’s gloom,
From St. Edmond’s shrine, lo! a skeleton’s hand,
Points to the black marble tomb.

text:
I would not dare to leave my friend,
For if he should die while I was gone,
And I, too late, should come at last
To the heart that had been waiting for me;
source_text:
I should not dare to leave my friend,
Because -- because if he should die
While I was gone, and I -- too late --
Should reach the heart that wanted me;

text:
Among those wild mountains my path shall still be,
Each stream foaming downward through its own green, narrow lea;
For there, with my lassie, I wander the long day,
While above us, unheeded, the swift hours of love pass away.
source_text:
Amang thae wild mountains shall still be my path,
Ilk stream foaming down its ain green, narrow strath;
For there, wi’ my lassie, the day lang I rove,
While o’er us unheeded flie the swift hours o’love.

text:
Words—book-words—what are you?
No words at all, for listen and look:
My song is out there in the open air, and I must sing,
With banner and pennant fluttering.
source_text:
Words! book-words! what are you?
Words no more, for hearken and see,
My song is there in the open air, and I must sing,
With the banner and pennant a-flapping.

text:
Another radiance kindled on his mouth,
That mouth from which there once was drawn the breath
That gave it force to pierce the guarded mind
And move into the heart that panted underneath
With lightning and with music: damp death
Had quenched its tenderness upon his icy lips;
And, like a falling star that brands a band
Of moonlit vapor, as the cold night grips,
It kindled through his pallid limbs, then passed into eclipse.
source_text:
Another Splendour on his mouth alit,
That mouth, whence it was wont to draw the breath
Which gave it strength to pierce the guarded wit,
And pass into the panting heart beneath
With lightning and with music: the damp death
Quenched its caress upon his icy lips;
And, as a dying meteor stains a wreath
Of moonlight vapour, which the cold night clips,
It flushed through his pale limbs, and passed to its eclipse.

text:
I see where Ernani walks the wedding garden,  
Through the perfume of night roses, shining, his bride clasped in his hand,  
And hears the hellish summons, the horn’s vow of death.
source_text:
I see where Ernani walking the bridal garden,
Amid the scent of night-roses, radiant, holding his bride by the hand,
Hears the infernal call, the death-pledge of the horn.

text:
So said he one bright morning; and through the day  
His heart beat heavily against his side;  
And to his heart he silently did pray  
For strength to speak; yet still the crimson tide  
Choked back his voice, and beat his purpose astray—  
It fevered all his proud thought of such a bride,  
Yet drew him down to meekness like a child:  
Alas! when passion is at once meek and wild!
source_text:
So said he one fair morning, and all day
His heart beat awfully against his side;
And to his heart he inwardly did pray
For power to speak; but still the ruddy tide
Stifled his voice, and puls'd resolve away--
Fever'd his high conceit of such a bride,
Yet brought him to the meekness of a child:
Alas! when passion is both meek and wild!

text:
FIRST VOICE (FROM THE MOUNTAINS):
For three times three hundred thousand years
we have stood above the earthquake’s couch;
and often, as men shook beneath their fears,
our countless multitude has shivered too.
source_text:
FIRST VOICE (FROM THE MOUNTAINS):
Thrice three hundred thousand years
O’er the Earthquake’s couch we stood:
Oft, as men convulsed with fears,
We trembled in our multitude.

text:
To tell if he was patient, partly content,  
Was dying as he thought, or something else meant;  
Was it a kindly day for him to die,  
And did the sunshine turn his way?
source_text:
To know if he was patient, part content,
Was dying as he thought, or different;
Was it a pleasant day to die,
And did the sunshine face his way?

text:
My lord, I know your gracious ear  
Finds no complaint in vain;  
That hope emboldens me to tell  
My lowly grief again,  
How saucy Phoebus’ scorching beams,  
In summer’s blazing pride,  
Wither and waste my foaming streams,  
And drain my crystal tide.
source_text:
My lord, I know your noble ear
Woe ne’er assails in vain;
Embolden’d thus, I beg you’ll hear
Your humble slave complain,
How saucy Phoebus’ scorching beams,
In flaming summer-pride,
Dry-withering, waste my foamy streams,
And drink my crystal tide.

text:
Old inheritances—these arts, libraries, legends, collections, and  
the craft handed down through the shops—shall we value them so high?  
Shall we value our money and business so high? I have no objection,  
I value them as high as the highest—yet a child born of woman and  
man I value beyond all measure.
source_text:
Old institutions, these arts, libraries, legends, collections, and
the practice handed along in manufactures, will we rate them so high?
Will we rate our cash and business high? I have no objection,
I rate them as high as the highest--then a child born of a woman and
man I rate beyond all rate.

text: In Book I we saw Hyperion, still a god, troubled by omens, and now in Book III we behold the rising into godhead of his heir, the young Apollo. The poem ends abruptly at the very instant of Apollo’s transformation, and how Keats meant to bring it to its close we can never know.
source_text:
In Book I we saw Hyperion, though still a god, distressed by portents,
and now in Book III we see the rise to divinity of his successor, the
young Apollo. The poem breaks off short at the moment of Apollo's
metamorphosis, and how Keats intended to complete it we can never know.

text:
An ancient tower rose up in thought,
And showed how Dettingen’s bold hero fought;
Yet, far from ever sinking into nought,
It still supports
A lord who in the western climes has fought
With his trusty sword.
source_text:
An ancient tower to memory brought
How Dettingen’s bold hero fought;
Still, far from sinking into nought,
It owns a lord
Who far in western climates fought,
With trusty sword.

text:
As I drifted out with life’s great tide,  
As I followed the coast I know so well,  
As I walked where the waves keep washing Paumanok,  
Where they hiss up rough and sibilant,  
Where the fierce old mother never stops calling for her lost ones,  
I, deep in thought on a late autumn afternoon, looking south,  
Held fast by this charged self, from whose pride I cast my poems,  
Was taken by the spirit that runs in the lines beneath my feet,  
The edge, the silt that speaks for all the water and all the land of the earth.
source_text:
As I ebb’d with the ocean of life,
As I wended the shores I know,
As I walk’d where the ripples continually wash you Paumanok,
Where they rustle up hoarse and sibilant,
Where the fierce old mother endlessly cries for her castaways,
I musing late in the autumn day, gazing off southward,
Held by this electric self out of the pride of which I utter poems,
Was seiz’d by the spirit that trails in the lines underfoot,
The rim, the sediment that stands for all the water and all the land
of the globe.

text:
Sharp as the arrows  
Of that silver orb,  
Whose fierce beam thins  
In the clear white dawn  
Until we can scarcely see it—we only feel it there.
source_text:
Keen as are the arrows
Of that silver sphere,
Whose intense lamp narrows
In the white dawn clear
Until we hardly see—we feel that it is there.

text:
Foreboding is the lengthening shadow on the lawn,
A sign that sinking suns have gone;
The message sent to startled grass
That darkness soon will sweep across.
source_text:
Presentiment is that long shadow on the lawn
Indicative that suns go down;
The notice to the startled grass
That darkness is about to pass.

text:
Farewell, my rhyme-making brother!
Too long have we been strangers to each other:
Now let us rest our minds together,
In brotherly devotion;
Let envy thrash about in tether,
That black infernal notion!
source_text:
Fareweel, “my rhyme-composing” brither!
We’ve been owre lang unkenn’d to ither:
Now let us lay our heads thegither,
In love fraternal:
May envy wallop in a tether,
Black fiend, infernal!

text:
“How could it be,” then Phoebus said,
“that you, a tiny child, born but yesterday,
a being nourished on a mother’s milk and kisses,
could skin two monstrous heifers with your hands?
Even I myself may one day have cause to fear
your power, child of Cyllenian May,
when you have grown robust and tall.” He spoke, and bound
the infant’s wrists with rigid willow bands.
source_text:
‘How was it possible,’ then Phoebus said,
‘That you, a little child, born yesterday,
A thing on mother’s milk and kisses fed,
Could two prodigious heifers ever flay?
Even I myself may well hereafter dread
Your prowess, offspring of Cyllenian May,
When you grow strong and tall.’—He spoke, and bound
Stiff withy bands the infant’s wrists around.

text:
Poet Willie, poet Willie, fire a volley at the Doctor,  
with your “Liberty’s Chain” and all your wit;  
you never swung a leg across Pegasus’ flank,  
you only caught the stench where he had sh—t.  
Poet Willie, you only caught the stench where he had sh—t.
source_text:
Poet Willie! poet Willie, gie the Doctor a volley,
Wi’ your “Liberty’s Chain” and your wit;
O’er Pegasus’ side ye ne’er laid a stride,
Ye but smelt, man, the place where he sh—t.
Poet Willie! Ye but smelt man, the place where he sh—t.

text:
I am the hunted slave, I flinch from the dogs’ savage bite,  
Hell and despair press hard on me, again and again the marksmen crack,  
I grip the fence rails, my blood drips thin with the seep of my skin,  
I sink among the weeds and stones,  
The riders drive their reluctant horses near, crowd close,  
Mock my spinning ears and strike me hard across the head with whip handles.
source_text:
I am the hounded slave, I wince at the bite of the dogs,
Hell and despair are upon me, crack and again crack the marksmen,
I clutch the rails of the fence, my gore dribs, thinn’d with the
ooze of my skin,
I fall on the weeds and stones,
The riders spur their unwilling horses, haul close,
Taunt my dizzy ears and beat me violently over the head with whip-stocks.

text:
Space is open and austere,  
And in its center, clouded, dim,  
A shrine of shadow stands,  
While, amid thrones of radiance,  
Darkness gathers in the blue of crystal air.  
Golden worlds turn and gleam.  

In winged flight,  
Out from each point of the Infinite,  
Like a thousand dawns within one night,  
The glories rise and spread;  
And through dread thunder and through darkness  
Light and music are shed,  
And in their canopied chariots borne,  
By living wings aloft above,  
The giant Powers advance,  
Gloomy or bright as the thrones they occupy.
source_text:
Space is roofless and bare,
And in the midst a cloudy shrine,
Dark amid thrones of light.
In the blue glow of hyaline
Golden worlds revolve and shine.
In ... flight
From every point of the Infinite,
Like a thousand dawns on a single night
The splendours rise and spread;
And through thunder and darkness dread
Light and music are radiated,
And in their pavilioned chariots led
By living wings high overhead
The giant Powers move,
Gloomy or bright as the thrones they fill.

text:
What joy can gold put in my hand?
I do not care a single strand;
The boy I love is all I need,
And he’s my own dear Willy.
source_text:
She. What’s a’ the joys that gowd can gie?
I dinna care a single flie;
The lad I love’s the lad for me,
And that’s my ain dear Willy.

text:
This dust was once the man,  
Kind, plain, just, and firm, beneath whose careful hand  
Against the foulest crime ever known in any land or age,  
The Union of these States was preserved.
source_text:
This dust was once the man,
Gentle, plain, just and resolute, under whose cautious hand,
Against the foulest crime in history known in any land or age,
Was saved the Union of these States.

text:
Her eyes were open, yet she still surveyed,  
Fully awake, the shapes her slumber made:  
A sorrowful change had almost driven out  
The dear delights her pure and deep dream knew throughout.  
Then fair Madeline began to weep,  
And, sighing many times, to murmur words asleep;  
Still fixed upon Porphyro was her sight;  
Who knelt there, hands together, with an eye of plight,  
Dreading to stir or speak, she seemed so strangely bright.
source_text:
Her eyes were open, but she still beheld,
Now wide awake, the vision of her sleep:
There was a painful change, that nigh expell'd
The blisses of her dream so pure and deep
At which fair Madeline began to weep,
And moan forth witless words with many a sigh;
While still her gaze on Porphyro would keep;
Who knelt, with joined hands and piteous eye,
Fearing to move or speak, she look'd so dreamingly.

text:
MEPHISTOPHELES [WHO AT ONCE SEEMS TO HAVE AGED GREATLY]:
I find the people ripe for judgment’s hour,
Since last I climbed the wizard mountain’s height;
And as my little barrel has turned cloudy,
So too the world is tapped down to the lees.
source_text:
MEPHISTOPHELES [WHO AT ONCE APPEARS TO HAVE GROWN VERY OLD]:
find the people ripe for the last day,
Since I last came up to the wizard mountain;
And as my little cask runs turbid now,
So is the world drained to the dregs.

text:
From the handwriting, it is never wholly clear which marks are dashes,  
which are commas, and which are full stops, nor is it fully  
clear which first letters begin in capitals.
source_text:
From the handwriting, it is not always clear which are dashes,
which are commas and which are periods, nor it is entirely
clear which initial letters are capitalized.

text:
May sorrow seize the man sae mean,  
Though bright his purse wi’ coin be clean,  
Wha follows ony saucy lass  
That walks sae proud and high.
source_text:
But sorrow tak’ him that’s sae mean,
Altho’ his pouch o’ coin were clean,
Wha follows ony saucy quean,
That looks sae proud and high.
O Tibbie, I hae seen the day, &c.

text:
And I have dreamed that the aim and heart of the life we know,  
the passing,  
is to shape and determine the self of the life unseen, the lasting.
source_text:
And I have dream’d that the purpose and essence of the known life,
the transient,
Is to form and decide identity for the unknown life, the permanent.

text:
Its eyes, though wild, were still all dewy-bright
with love, and kept all ghostly dread at bay
from the poor girl, by the spell of that light,
while it unwove the horrid web of the late
darkened days—the murderous spite of pride
and avarice—the black pine roof
in the forest, and the sodden, turf-clad dell,
where, wordless, from stabs he fell.
source_text:
Its eyes, though wild, were still all dewy bright
With love, and kept all phantom fear aloof
From the poor girl by magic of their light,
The while it did unthread the horrid woof
Of the late darken'd time,--the murderous spite
Of pride and avarice,--the dark pine roof
In the forest,--and the sodden turfed dell,
Where, without any word, from stabs he fell.

text:
MEPHISTOPHELES:
So be it then.
I have small misgiving for my wager,
And if I lose, then ’tis your turn to crow;
Then savor your triumph with a full breast.
Ay, dust he shall devour, and gladly so,
Like my old paramour, the renowned Snake.
source_text:
MEPHISTOPHELES:
Well and good.
I am not in much doubt about my bet,
And if I lose, then ’tis Your turn to crow;
Enjoy Your triumph then with a full breast.
Ay; dust shall he devour, and that with pleasure,
Like my old paramour, the famous Snake.

text:
Maybe you’re headed there too.
Who can say?
If you arrive before I do,
Keep a small place for me
Near the two I’ve lost!
source_text:
Perhaps you 're going too!
Who knows?
If you should get there first,
Save just a little place for me
Close to the two I lost!

text:
Each care and fear, when you are near,
I always brave them, O!
Young kings upon their throne at first
Are not so blessed as I am, O!
And I will kiss you yet, yet, &c.
source_text:
Ilk care and fear, when thou art near
I evermair defy them, O!
Young kings upon their hansel throne
Are no sae blest as I am, O!
And I’ll kiss thee yet, yet, &c.

text:
The man’s body is holy, and the woman’s body is holy,  
No matter who it is, it is holy—is it the lowest one in the  
crew of laborers?  
Is it one of the blank-faced immigrants newly come to the wharf?  
Each belongs here, or anywhere, as fully as the rich, as fully as  
you,  
Each has his or her place in the long procession.
source_text:
The man’s body is sacred and the woman’s body is sacred,
No matter who it is, it is sacred--is it the meanest one in the
laborers’ gang?
Is it one of the dull-faced immigrants just landed on the wharf?
Each belongs here or anywhere just as much as the well-off, just as
much as you,
Each has his or her place in the procession.

text: What deserves special notice is the deft use Keats makes here of contrast—between the merciless cold outside and the warmth of love within; between the trembling age of the Bedesman and Angela, and the ardent youth of Porphyro and Madeline; between the clamor and revelry and the stillness of Madeline’s chamber; and, as Mr. Colvin has observed, in the moonlight that, chill and sepulchral when it falls elsewhere, becomes for Madeline a halo of radiance, an angelic light.
source_text:
To be specially noted is the skilful use which Keats here makes of
contrast--between the cruel cold without and the warm love within; the
palsied age of the Bedesman and Angela, and the eager youth of Porphyro
and Madeline; the noise and revel and the hush of Madeline's bedroom,
and, as Mr. Colvin has pointed out, in the moonlight which, chill and
sepulchral when it strikes elsewhere, to Madeline is as a halo of glory,
an angelic light.

text:
Child of Light! your limbs are blazing, etc. (2 5 54.)
The first printed edition (1820) gives lips instead of limbs, but the word membre in Shelley’s Italian prose rendering of these lines confirms limbs, the reading of B. (Locock).
source_text:
Child of Light! thy limbs are burning, etc. (2 5 54.)
The editio princeps (1820) has lips for limbs, but the word membre in
Shelley’s Italian prose version of these lines establishes limbs, the
reading of B. (Locock).

text:
Think on it, lover! you and I  
Allowed to stand face to face;  
After a life, a death, we'll say—  
For that was death, and this is thee.
source_text:
Think of it, lover! I and thee
Permitted face to face to be;
After a life, a death we'll say, --
For death was that, and this is thee.

text:
For I, too, have forgotten,
wrapped in these small powers of progress, politics, culture,
wealth, invention, civilization,
I have lost my sense of your quiet, ever-swaying force, you
mighty, elemental surges,
in which, and upon which, we drift, and each of us is borne aloft.
source_text:
For I too have forgotten,
(Wrapt in these little potencies of progress, politics, culture,
wealth, inventions, civilization,)
Have lost my recognition of your silent ever-swaying power, ye
mighty, elemental throes,
In which and upon which we float, and every one of us is buoy’d.

text:
He saw his mystic friend’s whole being quiver,
Even in those innermost, most shadowed deeps—
And with a calm, deliberate voice he spake,
source_text:
Beheld his mystic friend’s whole being shake,
Even where its inmost depths were gloomiest—
And with a calm and measured voice he spake,

text:
Hail, Poetry, shy nymph apart!  
In chasing you, how many have gone astray  
from common sense, or slowly worn themselves thin  
amid the heaps of idle talk;  
and oh, how often your own joys have starved  
even as you kept on giving all your favors!
source_text:
Hail, Poesie! thou Nymph reserv’d!
In chase o’ thee, what crowds hae swerv’d
Frae common sense, or sunk enerv’d
’Mang heaps o’ clavers:
And och! o’er aft thy joes hae starv’d,
’Mid a’ thy favours!

text:
By that last book of yours, we think,
you’ve damned yourself twice over to contempt;
we warned you while you still stood on the brink,
and from your blackened name will shrink
the child not yet born.
source_text:
‘By that last book of yours WE think
You’ve double damned yourself to scorn;
We warned you whilst yet on the brink
You stood. From your black name will shrink
The babe that is unborn.’

text:
Stroke the cool brow, so oft made burning,
Lift, if you can, the languid hair;
Feel the hard and stubborn fingers—
No thimble more shall ever wear.
source_text:
Stroke the cool forehead, hot so often,
Lift, if you can, the listless hair;
Handle the adamantine fingers
Never a thimble more shall wear.

text:
For my friends and for my brethren’s sake,  
And for my dear-loved Land o’ Cakes,  
I lift this prayer in flame:  
Lord, send a rough-hoofed troop from Hell  
Across all those who’d Scotland buy or sell,  
To trample them in the mire!
source_text:
Now, for my friends’ and brethren’s sakes,
And for my dear-lov’d Land o’ Cakes,
I pray with holy fire:
Lord, send a rough-shod troop o’ Hell
O’er a’ wad Scotland buy or sell,
To grind them in the mire!

They glide, like phantoms, into the wide hall;
Like phantoms, to the iron porch, they glide;
Where lay the Porter, in uneasy sprawl,
With a huge empty flaggon by his side:
The wakeful bloodhound rose, and shook his hide,
But his sagacious eye an inmate owns:
By one, and one, the bolts full easy slide:--
The chains lie silent on the footworn stones;--
The key turns, and the door upon its hinges groans.

text:
The leaves of spent autumnal woods shall wander round thy head;  
The blossoms of the dewy spring shall glisten at thy feet:  
Yet thou or this old world must sink beneath the frost that binds the dead,  
Ere midnight wears its frown away, ere morning smiles, ere thou and peace may meet.
source_text:
The leaves of wasted autumn woods shall float around thine head:
The blooms of dewy spring shall gleam beneath thy feet:
But thy soul or this world must fade in the frost that binds the dead,
Ere midnight’s frown and morning’s smile, ere thou and peace may meet.
